refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve a measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target for the load→travel→dump cycle under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, with cycle time performance monitored and reported via telemetry.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-002 is a key performance indicator (KPI) requirement that directly impacts customer productivity and system acceptance. Refinement adds explicit reference to measurement/monitoring (telemetry) and duty cycle context to make verification concrete. High safety criticality tailoring requires that cycle time optimization does not compromise safety interlocks or emergency response.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrumented field trials under representative duty cycle; p95 cycle time computed from telemetry logs; comparison against CT_target acceptance threshold; safety event correlation analysis to ensure no safety compromise.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The vehicle shall execute load, travel, and dump sub-cycles with deterministic timing within design envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The teleoperation system shall introduce latency and control overhead not exceeding allocated cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: Safety services shall not introduce blocking delays that degrade cycle time below target, except during safety-critical interventions.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor and report cycle time metrics via telemetry for performance tracking.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Optimize vehicle hydraulic/mechanical actuation timing; minimize travel path via route planning; parallelize load/dump operations where safe.
DP-B: Implement predictive cycle time modeling with real-time adjustment of operational parameters (speed, acceleration profiles) based on site conditions and duty cycle.
DP-C: Establish teleoperation control architecture with low-latency network link and deterministic command processing; allocate cycle time budget across vehicle, teleoperation, and safety subsystems.
DP-D: Integrate safety services as non-blocking monitors with asynchronous alert generation; reserve hard real-time safety interlocks only for critical failure modes.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C + DP-D (integrated): Establish a hierarchical cycle time budget allocation across Vehicle (mechanical/hydraulic actuation), Teleoperation (control latency), and Safety Services (monitoring with non-blocking alert architecture). Safety interlocks are hard real-time only for critical failure modes; routine safety checks are asynchronous to preserve cycle time performance.

potential_sources: Coupling between teleoperation latency and vehicle actuation timing: mitigated by explicit budget allocation and deterministic control loop design.
Coupling between safety monitoring and cycle time: mitigated by separating hard real-time interlocks (blocking) from asynchronous safety alerts (non-blocking).
Coupling between site conditions (duty cycle) and achievable cycle time: acknowledged; DP includes real-time monitoring and reporting to enable adaptive operation within safety envelope.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP decouples safety criticality from cycle time optimization by design: safety is enforced via hard interlocks (non-negotiable), while cycle time is optimized within the safety-constrained envelope. This is a sound axiomatic design principle.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network link latency is predictable and bounded; risk: variable network conditions may violate latency budget.
Assumption: Vehicle actuation timing is deterministic; risk: hydraulic/mechanical variability under load may introduce jitter.
Assumption: Safety services can be architected as non-blocking monitors; risk: some safety checks may require synchronous validation, reducing cycle time headroom.
Assumption: Site duty cycle is well-characterized; risk: unexpected operational patterns may exceed design envelope.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify latency budget allocation (e.g., teleoperation ≤ X ms, vehicle actuation ≤ Y ms, safety monitoring ≤ Z ms) and define fallback behavior if any subsystem exceeds budget. Add DP-B (predictive modeling) as a secondary optimization layer to adapt to site-specific conditions.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Top-level system integration module responsible for cycle time KPI achievement. Owns the hierarchical budget allocation across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. Coordinates telemetry collection and performance reporting.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Cycle time metrics (load start, travel start, dump start, dump end timestamps) reported to Site Manager and System Engineer for KPI tracking and performance analysis.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operational commands (load, travel, dump) from Site Manager or Tele-operator; includes duty cycle context and site conditions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety events and alerts generated by Safety Services; non-blocking alerts do not interrupt cycle execution; hard interlocks trigger immediate operational halt.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for teleoperation control commands and telemetry feedback; latency and reliability directly impact cycle time performance.

module_containment_scores.notes: Cycle time KPI is a system-level performance metric that inherently belongs at the NGHE Solution level. All sub-components (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) contribute to this KPI, making NGHE Solution the natural owner of budget allocation and integration. Minimal diffusion risk.

Vehicle owns mechanical/hydraulic actuation timing, which is a major contributor to cycle time. However, cycle time KPI also depends on teleoperation latency and safety monitoring overhead, which are outside Vehicle scope. Assigning IM to Vehicle alone would create diffusion of cycle time responsibility.

Teleoperation owns control latency and command processing, which is a significant cycle time factor. However, it does not own vehicle actuation or safety services. Assigning IM to Teleoperation alone would fragment cycle time accountability.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and alert generation, which can impact cycle time if not architected carefully. However, Safety Services is not responsible for vehicle actuation or teleoperation control. Assigning IM to Safety Services alone would misalign ownership with the KPI.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate IM boundary because cycle time KPI is a system-level performance metric that requires coordinated optimization across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. NGHE Solution owns the budget allocation strategy, integration logic, and telemetry reporting. Sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) own their respective contributions to the budget.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'thin integration layer' if budget allocation logic is not sufficiently detailed; mitigation: define explicit budget allocation rules and fallback strategies at NGHE Solution level.

Risk: Cycle time optimization may inadvertently compromise safety if safety services are not properly integrated; mitigation: enforce hard real-time safety interlocks at NGHE Solution level; non-blocking alerts do not interrupt cycle execution.
Risk: Telemetry reporting may introduce overhead that degrades cycle time; mitigation: design telemetry collection as asynchronous, non-blocking process.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the cycle time budget allocation table at NGHE Solution level (e.g., Vehicle actuation: 40% of CT_target, Teleoperation latency: 30%, Safety monitoring: 20%, margin: 10%). Specify the decision logic for when to halt cycle execution due to safety events vs. when to continue with asynchronous alerts. Clarify ownership of telemetry collection and reporting within NGHE Solution.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR clearly specifies the cycle time KPI (p95 ≤ CT_target) with measurement and monitoring context. The selected DP (hierarchical budget allocation + non-blocking safety architecture) directly addresses the FR by decomposing cycle time optimization into manageable sub-problems (vehicle actuation, teleoperation latency, safety monitoring). The DP is logically sound and decouples safety from performance optimization. No independence axiom violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If budget allocation logic is not clearly owned by NGHE Solution, responsibility for cycle time may diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services without clear accountability.
Diffusion risk: If telemetry collection is not explicitly assigned to NGHE Solution, cycle time metrics may be computed inconsistently across subsystems.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary with containment score 95. The module owns system-level budget allocation, integration logic, and telemetry reporting. Sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) own their respective budget contributions. Interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and align with NGHE Solution scope.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Cycle time KPI is directly measurable via telemetry (timestamps for load, travel, dump events). p95 statistic can be computed from field trial data. Verification is feasible and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Cycle time responsibility is clearly allocated to NGHE Solution with explicit sub-allocations to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services via budget table. Ownership is unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Refine the cycle time budget allocation table with specific time allocations (e.g., Vehicle: 40 s, Teleoperation: 10 s, Safety: 5 s, margin: 5 s for CT_target = 60 s).
Define fallback behavior if any subsystem exceeds its budget (e.g., alert operator, reduce speed, halt cycle).
Specify telemetry collection frequency and timestamp precision to ensure p95 computation accuracy.
Document the safety interlock strategy: which failure modes trigger hard real-time blocking vs. asynchronous alerts.

decision.justification: The refined FR, selected DP, and assigned IM are logically sound and pass all gate reviews. However, the DP and IM are still at a high level of abstraction. To proceed with detailed design and implementation, we must decompose the sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-002.1 through FR-NGHE-002.4) into their respective DPs and IMs. This will clarify the budget allocation strategy, teleoperation latency budget, safety interlock logic, and telemetry architecture. Zigzag down to the next level to refine these sub-systems.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The vehicle shall execute load, travel, and dump sub-cycles with deterministic timing within design envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The teleoperation system shall introduce latency and control overhead not exceeding allocated cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: Safety services shall not introduce blocking delays that degrade cycle time below target, except during safety-critical interventions.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor and report cycle time metrics via telemetry for performance tracking.

assumptions: Network link latency is predictable and bounded within design envelope.
Vehicle actuation timing is deterministic under normal operating conditions.
Safety services can be architected with non-blocking asynchronous alerts for routine checks and hard real-time interlocks only for critical failure modes.
Site duty cycle is well-characterized and communicated to the NGHE solution at task initiation.
Telemetry collection and p95 computation do not introduce significant overhead that degrades cycle time.
Customer-defined CT_target is achievable within the constraints of vehicle mechanics, network latency, and safety requirements.
open_risks: Variable network conditions may violate teleoperation latency budget; mitigation: implement adaptive control with fallback to reduced-speed operation.
Hydraulic/mechanical variability under load may introduce jitter in vehicle actuation timing; mitigation: characterize variability in field trials and adjust budget margins.

Safety services may require synchronous validation for some checks, reducing cycle time headroom; mitigation: prioritize asynchronous monitoring; reserve synchronous checks for critical failure modes only.
Unexpected operational patterns at site may exceed design envelope; mitigation: implement real-time monitoring and adaptive operation within safety envelope.
Telemetry overhead may degrade cycle time if not carefully architected; mitigation: design telemetry as asynchronous, non-blocking process with configurable reporting frequency.
evidence_needed: Detailed cycle time budget allocation table with time allocations for Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and margin.
Network latency characterization under representative site conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss).
Vehicle actuation timing characterization (determinism, variability under load, response time).
Safety interlock strategy document: which failure modes trigger hard real-time blocking vs. asynchronous alerts.
Telemetry architecture design: collection frequency, timestamp precision, reporting mechanism, overhead analysis.
Field trial results: p95 cycle time under agreed duty cycle, safety event correlation, performance vs. CT_target.
